sections.header: Verses 1–18 — “The Tree That Reached to the Sky”

heb.paragraph: "I saw a tree (ʾilān) in the middle of the land. Its height was enormous. Its top touched the sky" (vv.10–11). Nebuchadnezzar’s own testimony in first person. He dreams of an enormous tree sheltering all creatures, then a heavenly messenger commands: "Cut it down! But let the stump remain, bound with iron and bronze, till he knows that the Most High is sovereign" (vv.14–17).

refs.note: Assyria as a great cedar, chopped down for pride — the same tree-empire metaphor applied to a different empire.
No one from east, west, or south can exalt themselves; God brings one down, he exalts another.
He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble — Mary’s Magnificat echoes Dan 4.

notes.note: Nebuchadnezzar writes in first person: his testimony. The king becomes a witness.
The tree: enormous, beautiful, sheltering everything. Empires shelter and provide, but they also claim the sky.
"Let the stump remain. Till he knows that the Most High is sovereign." The punishment is pedagogical.
"The Most High is sovereign over all kingdoms." The thesis restated. God assigns political power.
Calvin marvels that God converts the destroyer of Jerusalem into a witness. If Nebuchadnezzar can praise God, no one is beyond reach.
Calvin reads the stump-with-bands as the preservation of Nebuchadnezzar’s dynasty during his madness. God protects the kingdom for the king’s return — judgment tempered by mercy.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that the "watcher, a holy one" (Aramaic ʿir) is an angelic being. The term appears only in Daniel and later became important in 1 Enoch’s angelology.
The NET observes that "the Most High" (ʿillāyʾâ) is the primary divine name in chs.2–7 (Aramaic section), emphasising God’s supremacy over all gods and kingdoms.
Longman: the tree’s shade and food represent the genuine blessings of empire — Babylon provided order, trade, and security. The problem is not the tree itself but the tree’s claim to be self-planted.
Longman: "He gives kingdoms to anyone he wishes, even the lowliest of people." This is the most politically subversive sentence in the book — imperial power is a gift, not an achievement.

Goldingay: the first-person narration gives the chapter an autobiographical quality unique in Scripture. We hear the king’s own voice describing his own humiliation. The testimony-genre is Nebuchadnezzar’s final contribution to the book.
Goldingay notes the iron-and-bronze bands on the stump echo the metals of the statue in ch.2 — the empire is bound but not destroyed. The stump preserves hope.

Collins notes the closest ancient parallel: the Prayer of Nabonidus (4Q242) from Qumran, in which a Babylonian king is struck with disease for seven years and healed by a Jewish exorcist. This may preserve an older version of the tradition.
Collins reads the seven "times" (periods) as symbolic: seven = completeness. The madness lasts precisely as long as divine pedagogy requires.

hist.context: This chapter is unique in the OT: a pagan king writes his own testimony of conversion. The tree-as-empire metaphor appears in Ezekiel 31 (Assyria as a cedar) and in Jesus’ mustard seed parable (Matt 13:31–32). The medical condition described (living outdoors, eating grass, unkempt hair and nails) has been identified with boanthropy or clinical lycanthropy. Babylonian records have a gap in Nebuchadnezzar’s reign that some scholars connect to this episode.

ane.parallel: Prayer of Nabonidus (Qumran, 4Q242, 1st cent. BCE copy)
ane.similarity: This Dead Sea Scroll fragment describes a Babylonian king named Nabonidus who was afflicted with a severe skin disease for seven years while at Tema in Arabia. A Jewish exorcist helped him recover and instructed him to give glory to the Most High God. The structural parallel to Nebuchadnezzar's seven-year madness in Daniel 4 is striking.
ane.difference: The Qumran text names Nabonidus, the historical last king of Babylon, while Daniel 4 attributes the story to Nebuchadnezzar. The nature of the affliction differs (skin disease versus mental illness), and Daniel 4 is theologically developed with Nebuchadnezzar's dramatic confession of Yahweh's sovereignty.
ane.significance: The parallel suggests a historical tradition about a Babylonian king's humiliation and conversion that circulated in different forms. Daniel 4's attribution to Nebuchadnezzar and its theological elaboration may reflect editorial shaping of older material for didactic purposes.

sections.header: Verses 19–37 — “Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, Praise the King of Heaven”

heb.paragraph: "Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and exalt and glorify the King of heaven (ʿillāyʾâ), because everything he does is right and all his ways are just. Those who walk in pride he is able to humble" (v.37). Twelve months later Nebuchadnezzar boasts from his palace roof. Immediately a voice from heaven: your kingdom is departed. Driven from people, eating grass, hair like eagle feathers, nails like bird claws. After "seven times," he looks up — sanity restored.

refs.note: Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall — the proverb Daniel 4 dramatises.
God opposes the proud but shows favour to the humble — the NT summary of Daniel 4’s theology.
God exalted him to the highest place — the pattern: humbling precedes exaltation, for Christ and for Nebuchadnezzar.

notes.note: Daniel’s counsel: renounce your sins by doing what is right, and your wickedness by being kind to the oppressed.
"Is not this great Babylon I built?" The boast triggers the fall. Pride literally precedes the fall.
Grass, eagle-feather hair, bird-claw nails. Then: "I raised my eyes toward heaven, and my sanity was restored." Direction of gaze = everything.
"Those who walk in pride he is able to humble." The last sentence of the king’s testimony.
Calvin notes the connection between speech and judgment: the boast is still "on his lips" when the verdict falls. God times judgment to coincide with the sin’s fullest expression.

Calvin reads the restoration as genuine conversion: Nebuchadnezzar’s final confession is not diplomatic flattery but heartfelt worship. The most powerful man on earth ends his story by praising the God of a conquered people.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the archaeological confirmation: Nebuchadnezzar’s own inscriptions boast extensively about his building projects in Babylon. The roof-top boast fits the historical Nebuchadnezzar perfectly.
The NET observes the sequence: eyes raised → reason restored → praise offered. Physical posture precedes mental healing precedes theological confession.
Longman: the transition from boast to beast is the book’s most dramatic reversal. The king who claimed to be the highest is made the lowest. The king who built Babylon is sent to the fields.

Longman: the final verse functions as Nebuchadnezzar’s creed: God’s acts are right, his ways are just, and he humbles the proud. Three theological affirmations that summarise the first half of Daniel.

Goldingay: the upward gaze is the turning point not just of the chapter but of the entire court-tales section. Nebuchadnezzar’s trajectory — from threatening God’s servants (ch.1) to worshipping God (ch.4) — is the conversion arc the book exists to narrate.
Goldingay notes that Nebuchadnezzar’s final words in Daniel are about pride. The book’s diagnosis of empire: its core pathology is self-exaltation.

Collins notes that boanthropy (believing oneself to be an animal) is a documented clinical condition. Whether the account preserves a historical memory of Nebuchadnezzar’s illness or is a theological parable, the message is identical: no human ruler is exempt from divine humbling.
Collins reads v.37 as the climax of the court-tales section (chs.1–6): the most powerful human on earth acknowledges the Most High. The contest between human and divine sovereignty is decided.

hist.context: The boast-judgment sequence (vv.30–31) is instantaneous: the words are still on his lips when the voice speaks. The twelve-month delay (v.29) represents God’s patience — a year to repent before the sentence executes. The restoration (v.34) pivots on direction: Nebuchadnezzar "raised his eyes toward heaven." Looking DOWN = madness; looking UP = restoration. The chapter’s final verse (v.37) is the most extensive confession of God’s sovereignty by any pagan ruler in the OT.

ppl.text: 29) represents God’s patience — a year to repent before the sentence executes…

ppl.text: This chapter is unique in the OT: a pagan king writes his own testimony of conversion…

ppl.text: Nebuchadnezzar writes in first person: his testimony…

src.title: The Prayer of Nabonidus (4Q242, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A fragmentary Qumran text describes a Babylonian king struck with illness for seven years who is healed by a Jewish exorcist — strikingly parallel to Nebuchadnezzar’s madness in Daniel 4.
src.note: The Prayer of Nabonidus may preserve an older version of the tradition behind Daniel 4, or reflect a common Near Eastern motif of royal humbling. Either way, it confirms the antiquity of the Daniel traditions.

src.title: 1 Maccabees 1–2 (2nd c. BC)
src.quote: Describes the historical desecration of the Jerusalem temple by Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 167 BC — the event Daniel 8:9–14 and 11:31 prophesy as the “abomination of desolation.”
src.note: The fulfilment is documented in non-biblical Jewish sources, confirming Daniel’s prophetic precision whether the prophecy is dated to the 6th or 2nd century BC.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 10.11 (1st c. AD)
src.quote: Josephus records that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem (332 BC), the high priest showed him Daniel’s prophecy about a Greek king conquering Persia, and Alexander was so impressed he offered sacrifices at the temple.
src.note: Whether historically accurate or legendary, Josephus’s account shows that Daniel’s prophecies were understood as referring to Greek conquest well before the Christian era.

rec.text: "Nebuchadnezzar writes in first person: his testimony. The king becomes a witness."

rows.label: The Tree Dream — 4:1–18
rows.text: Nebuchadnezzar’s testimony. Enormous tree. "Till he knows the Most High is SOVEREIGN."

hebtext.note: "I saw a tree (ʾilān) in the middle of the land. Its height was enormous. Its top touched the sky" (vv.10–11). Nebuchadnezzar’s own testimony in first person. He dreams of an enormous tree sheltering all creatures, then a heavenly messenger commands: "Cut it down! But let the stump remain, bound with iron and bronze, till he knows that the Most High is sovereign" (vv.14–17).

"Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and exalt and glorify the King of heaven (ʿillāyʾâ), because everything he does is right and all his ways are just. Those who walk in pride he is able to humble" (v.37). Twelve months later Nebuchadnezzar boasts from his palace roof. Immediately a voice from heaven: your kingdom is departed. Driven from people, eating grass, hair like eagle feathers, nails like bird claws. After "seven times," he looks up — sanity restored.

thread.text: Assyria as a great cedar, chopped down for pride — the same tree-empire metaphor applied to a different empire.
No one from east, west, or south can exalt themselves; God brings one down, he exalts another.
He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble — Mary’s Magnificat echoes Dan 4.
Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall — the proverb Daniel 4 dramatises.
God opposes the proud but shows favour to the humble — the NT summary of Daniel 4’s theology.
God exalted him to the highest place — the pattern: humbling precedes exaltation, for Christ and for Nebuchadnezzar.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: Nebuchadnezzar’s madness and restoration. The king’s boast triggers the fall; his upward gaze triggers restoration.
